review_md:
## Referee report

### Overall verdict

I find the mathematical argument in `answer.tex` essentially correct and complete for the stated, explicitly adopted interpretation of \(R_n^{(m)}\) as the diagonal coinvariant algebra. The proof relies on two substantial external results—diagonal supersymmetry and the Rhoades–Wilson Hilbert series formula—but these are appropriate cited inputs, and the subsequent extraction of the hook coefficients is rigorous.

The LaTeX submission also satisfies the stated First Proof LaTeX contract: it uses exactly `\documentclass[12pt]{article}`, only the permitted `fullpage` layout package, no forbidden line-spacing or margin changes, no in-document font-size commands, and it compiles successfully to a 3-page PDF.

### Literature validation

The interpretation as a multivariate diagonal coinvariant setting is consistent with Bergeron’s work, whose abstract says the Hilbert series of diagonally harmonic/coinvariant spaces in multiple variable sets admits a global description independent of the number of sets. ([sciencedirect.com](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S000187081300056X))

The diagonal supersymmetry input is also supported by Lentfer’s stated theorem: for \(R_n^{(k,j)}\), the Frobenius series decomposes as a sum of super Schur functions \(s_\lambda(q/u)\) times \(\mathfrak S_n\)-Frobenius characters with coefficients independent of \(k,j\). The same source defines the super Schur convention \(s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u)\), matching the positive-sign convention used in `answer.tex`. ([pages.uoregon.edu](https://pages.uoregon.edu/plhersh/CALICO/Lentfer-2026.pdf))

The Rhoades–Wilson formula used in the proof is correctly quoted: their Theorem 5.3 gives
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(SR_n;q,z)=\sum_{k=1}^n z^{n-k}[k]!_q\,\operatorname{Stir}_q(n,k),
\]
and the recursion for \(\operatorname{Stir}_q(n,k)\) appears in the introduction in the same form used by the author. ([cambridge.org](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/forum-of-mathematics-pi/article/hilbert-series-of-the-superspace-coinvariant-ring/D7F1C91BE28068FC384AFE85B6AED8B8)) ([cambridge.org](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/forum-of-mathematics-pi/article/hilbert-series-of-the-superspace-coinvariant-ring/D7F1C91BE28068FC384AFE85B6AED8B8))

### Paragraph-by-paragraph audit

#### Title and interpretation section

The adopted interpretation of \(R_n^{(m)}\) as
\[
\mathbb C[x_i^{(r)}:1\le i\le n,\ 1\le r\le m]/
\langle \mathbb C[x_i^{(r)}]^{\mathfrak S_n}_{+}\rangle
\]
with diagonal \(\mathfrak S_n\)-action is faithful to the problem statement. The notation is slightly compressed, because \(\mathbb C[x_i^{(r)}]^{\mathfrak S_n}_{+}\) should be understood as the positive-degree invariants in the full polynomial ring, but the surrounding text makes this clear.

The statement that \(c_\lambda(n)\) are stable \(GL_m\)-multiplicities is valid under the diagonal \(GL_m\)-action mixing the \(m\) alphabets. This is a standard representation-theoretic interpretation of Schur coefficients in the multigraded Hilbert series.

#### Ordered set partition model

The definition of \(\OP_{r,k}\) is precise. The inversion statistic
\[
i<j,\qquad i=\min B_m,\qquad j\in B_\ell,\qquad \ell<m
\]
is the usual inversion statistic on ordered set partitions used with the \(q\)-Stirling recurrence. The claim that for \(k=r\) it reduces to the usual inversion number of the corresponding permutation is correct.

#### The theorem statement

The theorem gives a clean combinatorial interpretation:
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\{\sigma\in \OP_{r,k}:\ 2\le r\le n,\ r-k=b+1,\ r\text{ not singleton},\ \inv(\sigma)=a-1\}.
\]
The indexing is consistent: after the later substitution \(r\mapsto r+1\), the exponent of \(z\) is \(r-k-1=b\), so \(r-k=b+1\). The edge case \(n=1\) is harmless: the sum is empty, and only \(c_\varnothing(1)=1\) remains.

#### First standard input: diagonal supersymmetry

This is the main nontrivial external input. The author does not prove it, but cites Lentfer and states the needed consequence correctly: the universal coefficients in the purely bosonic Schur expansion are the same universal coefficients appearing in the bosonic–fermionic super Schur expansion, after applying the dimension map to the \(\mathfrak S_n\)-Frobenius factor.

This paragraph would be improved by citing the precise theorem number, but mathematically it is not a fatal gap because the cited result directly supplies the needed stability/universality statement.

#### Super Schur specialization

The specialization
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/z)=q^a z^b+q^{a-1}z^{b+1}
\]
is correct for the positive super Schur convention used by Lentfer. Non-hook partitions vanish in one even and one odd variable because they lie outside the \((1,1)\)-hook. Hence
\[
H_n(q,z)=1+(q+z)\sum_{a\ge1,b\ge0}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^{a-1}z^b
\]
is correct. The constant \(1\) accounts for \(\lambda=\varnothing\).

#### Second standard input: Rhoades–Wilson Hilbert series

The formula
\[
H_n(q,z)=\sum_{k=1}^{n} z^{n-k}[k]!_q\operatorname{Stir}_q(n,k)
\]
and the \(q\)-Stirling recurrence are correctly stated. The definition
\[
F_{r,k}=[k]!_q\operatorname{Stir}_q(r,k)
\]
then gives
\[
F_{r,k}=[k]_qF_{r-1,k-1}+[k]_qF_{r-1,k},
\]
which is algebraically correct.

#### Derivation of the recurrence for \(H_n\)

The passage
\[
H_n=H_{n-1}+(q+z)\sum_{k=1}^{n-1}[k]_qF_{n-1,k}z^{n-1-k}
\]
is valid. The key identity is
\[
[k+1]_q=1+q[k]_q,
\]
and the reindexing of the first summation contributes exactly \(H_{n-1}\). Iterating from \(H_1=1\) gives
\[
\frac{H_n(q,z)-1}{q+z}
=
\sum_{r=1}^{n-1}\sum_{k=1}^{r}[k]_qF_{r,k}z^{r-k}.
\]
No divisibility issue remains because the recurrence explicitly proves the quotient is a polynomial.

#### Identification of \(F_{r,k}\) with ordered set partitions

The proof that
\[
F_{r,k}=\sum_{\tau\in\OP_{r,k}}q^{\inv(\tau)}
\]
is sound. Inserting the largest element \(r\) either as a singleton into a partition of \([r-1]\) with \(k-1\) blocks or into an existing block of a partition of \([r-1]\) with \(k\) blocks gives the two terms of the recurrence. If the final position is \(i\) from the left, the new inversions are exactly contributed by the minima of the blocks to the right, giving \(k-i\) new inversions and therefore a factor \([k]_q\).

A minor presentational improvement would be to explicitly mention the base case \(F_{1,1}=1\), or the empty ordered partition convention for \(F_{0,0}=1\), but the recurrence and initial condition are clear enough.

#### Restriction to non-singleton largest element

The restricted insertion argument is bijective: inserting \(r+1\) into one of the existing \(k\) blocks of \(\tau\in\OP_{r,k}\) produces exactly those \(\sigma\in\OP_{r+1,k}\) in which \(r+1\) is not a singleton. Deleting \(r+1\) from its block recovers \(\tau\). The inversion increment is again one of \(0,\dots,k-1\), so
\[
[k]_qF_{r,k}
=
\sum_{\substack{\sigma\in\OP_{r+1,k}\\ r+1\text{ not singleton}}}
q^{\inv(\sigma)}.
\]
The final substitution \(r+1\mapsto r\) yields precisely the theorem’s generating function.

#### Coefficient extraction

Extracting the coefficient of \(q^{a-1}z^b\) gives the stated combinatorial interpretation. This final step is valid because the monomials \(q^{a-1}z^b\) are linearly independent.

### Computational cross-checks

I independently enumerated ordered set partitions for small \(r,k\) and verified:

1. \(F_{r,k}\) from the \(q\)-Stirling recurrence agrees with the inversion enumerator over \(\OP_{r,k}\).
2. \((q+z)G_n=H_n-1\), where \(G_n\) is the proposed hook-coefficient generating function.
3. The theorem’s non-singleton-largest-element enumeration agrees with \(G_n\) for the tested values \(n\le 5\).

No discrepancies appeared.

### LaTeX contract check

`answer.tex` compiles with `pdflatex` without errors. The resulting PDF has 3 pages, so it is below the 12-page limit. The document uses exactly `\documentclass[12pt]{article}`, uses only `fullpage` for layout, and contains no forbidden margin, spacing, or font-size commands. The contract is satisfied.

### Final assessment

I found no mathematical error, no unresolved essential lemma, and no First Proof LaTeX contract violation. The only issues are minor expository ones: the heavy diagonal supersymmetry input should ideally be cited with a theorem number, and the ordered-partition recurrence could spell out the initial condition more explicitly. These do not affect correctness.

messages_after.content:
Act as a strict mathematical referee. Below you find a mathematical
problem statement together with an attempt at a solution. Perform an
in-depth review of this given answer, going paragraph by paragraph to
audit its validity. Check for any mathematical errors, gaps in given
arguments, missing assumptions when applying known results, handwaving,
unclear formulations, unproved essential lemmas, or unresolved
"Remaining open issues". Use web search to validate any cited results
from the literature, and perform cross-checks using code-interpreter
where appropriate. Your goal is to identify *any* issues which could
affect the mathematical validity of the given treatment. Then give me
a full report.

Set `<answer_ready>true</answer_ready>` only if answer.tex fully solves
the stated problem as a complete rigorous solution, with no remaining
open gaps, no unproved essential lemmas, and no missing assumptions. If
the problem statement was ambiguous, answer.tex must explicitly record
the adopted interpretation in a "Problem statement and interpretation"
section and solve that faithful interpretation. A partial final answer
that merely lists open issues is not answer-ready and must end with
`<answer_ready>false</answer_ready>`.

Also set `<answer_ready>false</answer_ready>` if answer.tex violates
the First Proof LaTeX contract supplied below: wrong document class,
font size other than 12pt, over the page limit, non-permitted
margin/layout changes, line-spacing changes, in-document font-size
changes, or any LaTeX compile failure.

End your report with exactly one of these two lines, on its own line, with no additional text:

- `<answer_ready>true</answer_ready>`
- `<answer_ready>false</answer_ready>`

# Problem statement

Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

# First Proof LaTeX contract

First Proof LaTeX submission contract:
- `answer.tex` must be a complete standalone LaTeX document.
- Use exactly `\documentclass[12pt]{article}`.
- The compiled PDF must be at most 12 pages.
- The `fullpage` package is permitted.
- Do not use any other margin/layout changes: no `geometry`, `a4wide`,
  `typearea`, `anysize`, manual `\textwidth`, `\textheight`,
  `\oddsidemargin`, `\evensidemargin`, `\topmargin`, etc.
- Do not change line spacing: no `setspace`, `\linespread`,
  `\baselinestretch`, `\onehalfspacing`, or `\doublespacing`.
- Do not change font size inside the document: no `\small`,
  `\footnotesize`, `\scriptsize`, or `\fontsize`.
- Run `pdflatex` and fix compile errors before claiming readiness.


# Author's solution attempt

## `answer.tex`

```latex
\documentclass[12pt]{article}
\usepackage{fullpage}
\usepackage{amsmath,amssymb,amsthm}

\newtheorem{theorem}{Theorem}
\newtheorem{lemma}{Lemma}

\newcommand{\OP}{\operatorname{OP}}
\newcommand{\inv}{\operatorname{inv}}
\newcommand{\Hilb}{\operatorname{Hilb}}
\newcommand{\Stir}{\operatorname{Stir}}

\begin{document}

\begin{center}
{\bf Hook coefficients in the universal Hilbert series of diagonal coinvariants}
\end{center}

\section*{Problem statement and interpretation}

I interpret \(R_n^{(m)}\) as the diagonal coinvariant algebra
\[
 R_n^{(m)}=\mathbb C[x_i^{(r)}:1\le i\le n,\ 1\le r\le m]\,
/\,\langle \mathbb C[x_i^{(r)}]^{\mathfrak S_n}_{+}\rangle,
\]
where \(\mathfrak S_n\) acts by simultaneously permuting the subscript \(i\) in all
\(m\) sets of variables.  Thus the coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) are the stable
multiplicities of the polynomial \(GL_m\)-irreducibles \(S_\lambda(\mathbb C^m)\).

\section*{The combinatorial model}

For \(r,k\ge 1\), let \(\OP_{r,k}\) be the set of ordered set partitions
\(\sigma=(B_1|\cdots|B_k)\) of \([r]\) into \(k\) nonempty blocks.  An inversion of
\(\sigma\) is a pair \((i,j)\) such that
\[
        i<j,\qquad i=\min B_m,\qquad j\in B_\ell,\qquad \ell<m .
\]
Let \(\inv(\sigma)\) be the number of such pairs.  When \(k=r\), this is the
usual inversion number of the permutation obtained by reading the singleton
blocks from left to right.

\begin{theorem}
Let \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\) be a hook, with \(a\ge 1\) and \(b\ge 0\).  Then
\(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\) is the number of ordered set partitions
\(\sigma=(B_1|\cdots|B_k)\) of \([r]\), for some \(2\le r\le n\), such that
\[
        r-k=b+1,\qquad r\hbox{ is not a singleton block of }\sigma,\qquad
        \inv(\sigma)=a-1 .
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0} c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\,q^{a-1}z^b
=
\sum_{\substack{2\le r\le n\\1\le k\le r-1}}
\ \sum_{\substack{\sigma\in \OP_{r,k}\\
                  r\text{ not singleton in }\sigma}}
        q^{\inv(\sigma)}z^{r-k-1}.
\]
The empty partition coefficient is \(c_{\varnothing}(n)=1\).
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
We use two standard inputs.  First, diagonal supersymmetry says that the
universal Schur coefficients for diagonal coinvariants can be read from the
coinvariant ring with one bosonic alphabet and one fermionic alphabet,
\[
 SR_n=\bigl(\mathbb C[x_1,\ldots,x_n]\otimes
          \bigwedge(\theta_1,\ldots,\theta_n)\bigr)
/\langle(\mathbb C[x]\otimes\bigwedge\theta)^{\mathfrak S_n}_{+}\rangle .
\]
More explicitly, the supersymmetric Frobenius/character expansion has the same
universal multiplicity modules as the purely bosonic expansion; after applying
the dimension map to the \(\mathfrak S_n\)-Frobenius factor one obtains exactly
the Hilbert coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) appearing in the question.  Therefore
\[
        \Hilb(SR_n;q,z)=\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q/z),
\]
where \(s_\lambda(q/z)\) denotes the super Schur polynomial in one even variable
\(q\) and one odd variable \(z\).  With one even and one odd variable,
\(s_\lambda(q/z)\) vanishes unless \(\lambda\) lies in the \((1,1)\)-hook, i.e.
unless \(\lambda\) is a hook, and
\[
        s_{(a,1^b)}(q/z)=q^a z^b+q^{a-1}z^{b+1}
        =q^{a-1}z^b(q+z).
\]
Thus
\[
        \Hilb(SR_n;q,z)
        =1+(q+z)\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0}
              c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^{a-1}z^b .          \tag{1}
\]

Second, the Hilbert series of \(SR_n\), due to Rhoades--Wilson, is
\[
        H_n(q,z):=\Hilb(SR_n;q,z)
        =\sum_{k=1}^{n} z^{\,n-k}[k]!_q\,\Stir_q(n,k),                 \tag{2}
\]
where \([k]_q=1+q+\cdots+q^{k-1}\), \([k]!_q=[k]_q[k-1]_q\cdots[1]_q\), and
\[
        \Stir_q(r,k)=\Stir_q(r-1,k-1)+[k]_q\Stir_q(r-1,k).
\]
The boundary conditions are
\(\Stir_q(0,0)=1\), \(\Stir_q(r,0)=0\) for \(r>0\), and
\(\Stir_q(r,k)=0\) outside \(0\le k\le r\).  Put
\(F_{r,k}=[k]!_q\Stir_q(r,k)\).  Then \(F_{r,k}=0\) outside
\(1\le k\le r\), except for the harmless convention \(F_{0,0}=1\), and
\[
        F_{r,k}=[k]_qF_{r-1,k-1}+[k]_qF_{r-1,k}.
\]
Using \([k+1]_q=1+q[k]_q\) in (2), we get, for \(n\ge2\),
\[
\begin{aligned}
H_n(q,z)
&=\sum_{k=1}^{n}z^{n-k}\bigl([k]_qF_{n-1,k-1}
                         +[k]_qF_{n-1,k}\bigr)\\
&=H_{n-1}(q,z)
 +(q+z)\sum_{k=1}^{n-1}[k]_qF_{n-1,k}z^{n-1-k}.
\end{aligned}
\]
Since \(H_1(q,z)=1\), iteration gives
\[
        \frac{H_n(q,z)-1}{q+z}
        =
        \sum_{r=1}^{n-1}\sum_{k=1}^{r}
             [k]_q\,F_{r,k}\,z^{r-k}.                    \tag{3}
\]

It remains to identify the right side of (3).  The polynomial \(F_{r,k}\) is
the inversion enumerator of ordered set partitions:
\[
        F_{r,k}=\sum_{\tau\in \OP_{r,k}}q^{\inv(\tau)}.                \tag{4}
\]
Indeed, both sides satisfy the same recurrence.  To see this directly, insert
the largest letter \(r\) into an ordered partition of \([r-1]\).  To obtain
\(k\) final blocks, either insert \(r\) as a singleton block into one of the
\(k\) possible final block positions, or insert \(r\) into one of the \(k\)
existing blocks.  If the final position is \(i\) from the left, the new
inversions involving \(r\) are exactly the minima of the blocks to its right, so
their number is \(k-i\).  Summing over \(i=1,\ldots,k\) contributes
\([k]_q\) in each of the two cases, giving the recurrence above and the initial
condition.

Now restrict this insertion argument to insertions of \(r+1\) into an existing
block of an ordered partition \(\tau\in\OP_{r,k}\).  The result is precisely an
ordered partition \(\sigma\in\OP_{r+1,k}\) in which \(r+1\) is not a singleton,
and the extra inversion contribution is again one of \(0,1,\ldots,k-1\).
Hence
\[
 [k]_qF_{r,k}
 =
 \sum_{\substack{\sigma\in\OP_{r+1,k}\\ r+1\text{ not singleton}}}
      q^{\inv(\sigma)} .
\]
Substituting this into (3), and replacing \(r+1\) by \(r\), gives exactly
\[
\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^{a-1}z^b
=
\sum_{\substack{2\le r\le n\\1\le k\le r-1}}
\sum_{\substack{\sigma\in \OP_{r,k}\\r\text{ not singleton}}}
q^{\inv(\sigma)}z^{r-k-1}.
\]
Extracting the coefficient of \(q^{a-1}z^b\) proves the claimed interpretation.
\end{proof}

\begin{thebibliography}{9}

\bibitem{Bergeron}
F. Bergeron,
\emph{Multivariate diagonal coinvariant spaces for complex reflection groups},
Adv. Math. 239 (2013), 97--108.

\bibitem{Lentfer}
J. Lentfer,
\emph{Diagonal supersymmetry for coinvariant rings},
arXiv:2505.14885 (2025).

\bibitem{RhoadesWilson}
B. Rhoades and A. T. Wilson,
\emph{The Hilbert series of the superspace coinvariant ring},
Forum Math. Pi 12 (2024), e16.

\end{thebibliography}

\end{document}

```

# Author's working notes (background context only)

This is the Author's persistent scratchpad — not the deliverable, and not the focus of your review. Skim it; flag any fatal mathematical error you happen to spot that could be steering the proof in a wrong direction. Otherwise concentrate the review on `answer.tex`.

## `research_notes.tex`

```latex
\documentclass[12pt]{article}
\usepackage{amsmath,amssymb}
\begin{document}
\section*{Research notes}

Problem interpreted as the diagonal coinvariant algebra
\[
R_n^{(m)}=\mathbb C[x_i^{(r)}:1\le i\le n,\ 1\le r\le m]/
\langle \mathbb C[x_i^{(r)}]^{\mathfrak S_n}_{+}\rangle ,
\]
where \(\mathfrak S_n\) simultaneously permutes the subscript \(i\).  The
universal Schur coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) are stable \(GL_m\)-multiplicities
in the multigraded Hilbert series.

Main reduction: use one bosonic and one fermionic alphabet.  Diagonal
supersymmetry gives the same universal multiplicity data for the superspace
coinvariant ring
\[
SR_n=(\mathbb C[x_1,\ldots,x_n]\otimes \bigwedge(\theta_1,\ldots,\theta_n))/
\langle(\mathbb C[x]\otimes\bigwedge\theta)^{\mathfrak S_n}_{+}\rangle .
\]
At Frobenius-character level the coefficients are \(\mathfrak S_n\)-modules (or
symmetric functions); applying the dimension map to the \(\mathfrak S_n\)-factor
recovers the Hilbert coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\).  Hence
\[
H_n(q,z):=\operatorname{Hilb}(SR_n;q,z)=\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q/z).
\]
Here \(s_\lambda(q/z)\) is the super Schur polynomial in one even variable \(q\)
and one odd variable \(z\).  Only partitions in the \((1,1)\)-hook survive, and
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/z)=q^az^b+q^{a-1}z^{b+1}=q^{a-1}z^b(q+z).
\]
Therefore
\[
H_n(q,z)=1+(q+z)\sum_{a\ge1,b\ge0}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^{a-1}z^b.
\]

Rhoades--Wilson Hilbert series:
\[
H_n(q,z)=\sum_{k=1}^n z^{n-k}[k]!_q\operatorname{Stir}_q(n,k),
\]
where
\[
\operatorname{Stir}_q(r,k)=\operatorname{Stir}_q(r-1,k-1)+[k]_q\operatorname{Stir}_q(r-1,k),
\]
with initial conditions \(\operatorname{Stir}_q(0,0)=1\),
\(\operatorname{Stir}_q(r,0)=0\) for \(r>0\), and vanishing outside
\(0\le k\le r\).  Set \(F_{r,k}=[k]!_q\operatorname{Stir}_q(r,k)\).  Then
\(F_{r,k}=0\) outside \(1\le k\le r\) (apart from \(F_{0,0}=1\)) and
\[
F_{r,k}=[k]_qF_{r-1,k-1}+[k]_qF_{r-1,k}.
\]
Using \([k+1]_q=1+q[k]_q\),
\[
H_n=H_{n-1}+(q+z)\sum_{k=1}^{n-1}[k]_qF_{n-1,k}z^{n-1-k},
\]
so, since \(H_1=1\),
\[
\frac{H_n-1}{q+z}=\sum_{r=1}^{n-1}\sum_{k=1}^r [k]_qF_{r,k}z^{r-k}.
\]

Combinatorial interpretation: \(F_{r,k}=\sum_{\sigma\in OP_{r,k}}q^{\operatorname{inv}(\sigma)}\), where an inversion of
\(\sigma=(B_1|\cdots|B_k)\) is a pair \(i<j\) with \(i=\min B_m\),
\(j\in B_\ell\), and \(\ell<m\).  Inserting the largest letter into final
position \(i\) creates \(k-i\) inversions.  The singleton insertion case and the
existing-block insertion case give the two recurrence terms.  Restricting to
insertion of \(r+1\) into an existing block gives
\[
[k]_qF_{r,k}=\sum_{\substack{\sigma\in OP_{r+1,k}\\r+1\text{ not singleton}}}
q^{\operatorname{inv}(\sigma)}.
\]
Consequently \(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\) counts ordered set partitions of \([r]\), with
\(2\le r\le n\), \(r-k=b+1\), largest element \(r\) not singleton, and
\(\operatorname{inv}=a-1\).  Also \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\).

Sanity checks from Python.  Let
\[
G_n(q,z)=\sum_{a\ge1,b\ge0}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^{a-1}z^b=(H_n(q,z)-1)/(q+z).
\]
For \(n=4\),
\[
G_4=q^5+3q^4+5q^3+6q^2+5q+3
+(q^3+4q^2+5q+3)z+z^2.
\]
For \(n=5\),
\[
\begin{aligned}
G_5={}&q^9+4q^8+9q^7+15q^6+20q^5+22q^4+20q^3+15q^2+9q+4\\
&+(q^7+5q^6+14q^5+24q^4+29q^3+26q^2+16q+6)z\\
&+(q^4+5q^3+10q^2+9q+4)z^2+z^3.
\end{aligned}
\]
Direct enumeration of ordered set partitions agrees with these polynomials.

References used in answer: Bergeron 2013 for the diagonal coinvariant setting
and universal bosonic Schur series; Lentfer 2025 for diagonal supersymmetry;
Rhoades--Wilson 2024 for the superspace coinvariant Hilbert series and the
\(q\)-Stirling formula.
\end{document}

```

## Referee report

### Overall verdict

I find the mathematical argument in `answer.tex` essentially correct and complete for the stated, explicitly adopted interpretation of \(R_n^{(m)}\) as the diagonal coinvariant algebra. The proof relies on two substantial external results—diagonal supersymmetry and the Rhoades–Wilson Hilbert series formula—but these are appropriate cited inputs, and the subsequent extraction of the hook coefficients is rigorous.

The LaTeX submission also satisfies the stated First Proof LaTeX contract: it uses exactly `\documentclass[12pt]{article}`, only the permitted `fullpage` layout package, no forbidden line-spacing or margin changes, no in-document font-size commands, and it compiles successfully to a 3-page PDF.

### Literature validation

The interpretation as a multivariate diagonal coinvariant setting is consistent with Bergeron’s work, whose abstract says the Hilbert series of diagonally harmonic/coinvariant spaces in multiple variable sets admits a global description independent of the number of sets. ([sciencedirect.com](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S000187081300056X))

The diagonal supersymmetry input is also supported by Lentfer’s stated theorem: for \(R_n^{(k,j)}\), the Frobenius series decomposes as a sum of super Schur functions \(s_\lambda(q/u)\) times \(\mathfrak S_n\)-Frobenius characters with coefficients independent of \(k,j\). The same source defines the super Schur convention \(s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u)\), matching the positive-sign convention used in `answer.tex`. ([pages.uoregon.edu](https://pages.uoregon.edu/plhersh/CALICO/Lentfer-2026.pdf))

The Rhoades–Wilson formula used in the proof is correctly quoted: their Theorem 5.3 gives
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(SR_n;q,z)=\sum_{k=1}^n z^{n-k}[k]!_q\,\operatorname{Stir}_q(n,k),
\]
and the recursion for \(\operatorname{Stir}_q(n,k)\) appears in the introduction in the same form used by the author. ([cambridge.org](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/forum-of-mathematics-pi/article/hilbert-series-of-the-superspace-coinvariant-ring/D7F1C91BE28068FC384AFE85B6AED8B8)) ([cambridge.org](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/forum-of-mathematics-pi/article/hilbert-series-of-the-superspace-coinvariant-ring/D7F1C91BE28068FC384AFE85B6AED8B8))

### Paragraph-by-paragraph audit

#### Title and interpretation section

The adopted interpretation of \(R_n^{(m)}\) as
\[
\mathbb C[x_i^{(r)}:1\le i\le n,\ 1\le r\le m]/
\langle \mathbb C[x_i^{(r)}]^{\mathfrak S_n}_{+}\rangle
\]
with diagonal \(\mathfrak S_n\)-action is faithful to the problem statement. The notation is slightly compressed, because \(\mathbb C[x_i^{(r)}]^{\mathfrak S_n}_{+}\) should be understood as the positive-degree invariants in the full polynomial ring, but the surrounding text makes this clear.

The statement that \(c_\lambda(n)\) are stable \(GL_m\)-multiplicities is valid under the diagonal \(GL_m\)-action mixing the \(m\) alphabets. This is a standard representation-theoretic interpretation of Schur coefficients in the multigraded Hilbert series.

#### Ordered set partition model

The definition of \(\OP_{r,k}\) is precise. The inversion statistic
\[
i<j,\qquad i=\min B_m,\qquad j\in B_\ell,\qquad \ell<m
\]
is the usual inversion statistic on ordered set partitions used with the \(q\)-Stirling recurrence. The claim that for \(k=r\) it reduces to the usual inversion number of the corresponding permutation is correct.

#### The theorem statement

The theorem gives a clean combinatorial interpretation:
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\{\sigma\in \OP_{r,k}:\ 2\le r\le n,\ r-k=b+1,\ r\text{ not singleton},\ \inv(\sigma)=a-1\}.
\]
The indexing is consistent: after the later substitution \(r\mapsto r+1\), the exponent of \(z\) is \(r-k-1=b\), so \(r-k=b+1\). The edge case \(n=1\) is harmless: the sum is empty, and only \(c_\varnothing(1)=1\) remains.

#### First standard input: diagonal supersymmetry

This is the main nontrivial external input. The author does not prove it, but cites Lentfer and states the needed consequence correctly: the universal coefficients in the purely bosonic Schur expansion are the same universal coefficients appearing in the bosonic–fermionic super Schur expansion, after applying the dimension map to the \(\mathfrak S_n\)-Frobenius factor.

This paragraph would be improved by citing the precise theorem number, but mathematically it is not a fatal gap because the cited result directly supplies the needed stability/universality statement.

#### Super Schur specialization

The specialization
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/z)=q^a z^b+q^{a-1}z^{b+1}
\]
is correct for the positive super Schur convention used by Lentfer. Non-hook partitions vanish in one even and one odd variable because they lie outside the \((1,1)\)-hook. Hence
\[
H_n(q,z)=1+(q+z)\sum_{a\ge1,b\ge0}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^{a-1}z^b
\]
is correct. The constant \(1\) accounts for \(\lambda=\varnothing\).

#### Second standard input: Rhoades–Wilson Hilbert series

The formula
\[
H_n(q,z)=\sum_{k=1}^{n} z^{n-k}[k]!_q\operatorname{Stir}_q(n,k)
\]
and the \(q\)-Stirling recurrence are correctly stated. The definition
\[
F_{r,k}=[k]!_q\operatorname{Stir}_q(r,k)
\]
then gives
\[
F_{r,k}=[k]_qF_{r-1,k-1}+[k]_qF_{r-1,k},
\]
which is algebraically correct.

#### Derivation of the recurrence for \(H_n\)

The passage
\[
H_n=H_{n-1}+(q+z)\sum_{k=1}^{n-1}[k]_qF_{n-1,k}z^{n-1-k}
\]
is valid. The key identity is
\[
[k+1]_q=1+q[k]_q,
\]
and the reindexing of the first summation contributes exactly \(H_{n-1}\). Iterating from \(H_1=1\) gives
\[
\frac{H_n(q,z)-1}{q+z}
=
\sum_{r=1}^{n-1}\sum_{k=1}^{r}[k]_qF_{r,k}z^{r-k}.
\]
No divisibility issue remains because the recurrence explicitly proves the quotient is a polynomial.

#### Identification of \(F_{r,k}\) with ordered set partitions

The proof that
\[
F_{r,k}=\sum_{\tau\in\OP_{r,k}}q^{\inv(\tau)}
\]
is sound. Inserting the largest element \(r\) either as a singleton into a partition of \([r-1]\) with \(k-1\) blocks or into an existing block of a partition of \([r-1]\) with \(k\) blocks gives the two terms of the recurrence. If the final position is \(i\) from the left, the new inversions are exactly contributed by the minima of the blocks to the right, giving \(k-i\) new inversions and therefore a factor \([k]_q\).

A minor presentational improvement would be to explicitly mention the base case \(F_{1,1}=1\), or the empty ordered partition convention for \(F_{0,0}=1\), but the recurrence and initial condition are clear enough.

#### Restriction to non-singleton largest element

The restricted insertion argument is bijective: inserting \(r+1\) into one of the existing \(k\) blocks of \(\tau\in\OP_{r,k}\) produces exactly those \(\sigma\in\OP_{r+1,k}\) in which \(r+1\) is not a singleton. Deleting \(r+1\) from its block recovers \(\tau\). The inversion increment is again one of \(0,\dots,k-1\), so
\[
[k]_qF_{r,k}
=
\sum_{\substack{\sigma\in\OP_{r+1,k}\\ r+1\text{ not singleton}}}
q^{\inv(\sigma)}.
\]
The final substitution \(r+1\mapsto r\) yields precisely the theorem’s generating function.

#### Coefficient extraction

Extracting the coefficient of \(q^{a-1}z^b\) gives the stated combinatorial interpretation. This final step is valid because the monomials \(q^{a-1}z^b\) are linearly independent.

### Computational cross-checks

I independently enumerated ordered set partitions for small \(r,k\) and verified:

1. \(F_{r,k}\) from the \(q\)-Stirling recurrence agrees with the inversion enumerator over \(\OP_{r,k}\).
2. \((q+z)G_n=H_n-1\), where \(G_n\) is the proposed hook-coefficient generating function.
3. The theorem’s non-singleton-largest-element enumeration agrees with \(G_n\) for the tested values \(n\le 5\).

No discrepancies appeared.

### LaTeX contract check

`answer.tex` compiles with `pdflatex` without errors. The resulting PDF has 3 pages, so it is below the 12-page limit. The document uses exactly `\documentclass[12pt]{article}`, uses only `fullpage` for layout, and contains no forbidden margin, spacing, or font-size commands. The contract is satisfied.

### Final assessment

I found no mathematical error, no unresolved essential lemma, and no First Proof LaTeX contract violation. The only issues are minor expository ones: the heavy diagonal supersymmetry input should ideally be cited with a theorem number, and the ordered-partition recurrence could spell out the initial condition more explicitly. These do not affect correctness.
